title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing — July 18, 2026
headline: Ukraine's first own-conduct entry: strikes on Russian warehouses kill seven night-shift workers.
summary: Fifteen countries held their published scores steady tonight, the seventh straight cycle without a single score change. Tonight's most important finding involves Ukraine, not Russia. Ukrainian drones struck two Russian logistics warehouses on July 18, killing nine people. Seven of the dead were civilian night-shift workers at a retail distribution center 500 to 700 kilometers from the front line. President Volodymyr Zelensky publicly acknowledged the strikes. This is the first time this benchmark has recorded Ukraine as the country responsible for a civilian-harm event, rather than the country harmed by one. Every earlier assessment of Ukraine recorded harm inflicted on Ukraine by Russia. Scored honestly against the benchmark's own anchors, tonight's finding is worth about 1.4 points on a 100-point scale. That is far short of the 5-point threshold the benchmark requires before it changes a published score. Ukraine holds at 50 of 100 with a downward watch. A second dated incident, or an international human-rights finding calling the strike unlawful, would convert this into a proposed score change. A second major finding involves the United States. Its published score of 17.5 was set on June 9 based entirely on domestic conduct -- before a US strike campaign against Iran began on July 6. That campaign, now in its third week, is real, confirmed, and never priced into the current score. Scored honestly, it is worth about 2.8 points -- still below the 5-point threshold, but the largest unconverted downward signal now open in the country index. Casualty figures come from Iran's own Health Ministry and are reported by international outlets as Iranian government claims, not independently verified counts. Israel, Sudan, Russia, and Afghanistan all held at zero, the lowest score the benchmark gives, each reinforced by fresh evidence this cycle. Myanmar held at zero as well, but a claimed massacre of 17 captured soldiers came from a single, uncorroborated newsletter. It was logged as an unverified watch item, not scored evidence. Afghanistan's zero score gained confirmed evidence for the first time in two cycles. Human Rights Watch documented that Taliban restrictions on women working in aid roles are worsening a hunger crisis affecting more than 17 million people. Iran, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Pakistan, Lebanon, China, Venezuela, Cuba, and South Sudan all confirmed their published scores without a new escalation large enough to move them. No new score changes were proposed tonight, and no proposals are waiting for a decision.

pipeline.queueHandling: No proposals are awaiting action tonight. No new proposals were generated this cycle, the seventh consecutive night without one.

topSignals.title: Ukraine Enters the Ledger as the Acting Party in a Civilian-Harm Event for the First Time
topSignals.whyItMatters: Every earlier finding on Ukraine recorded harm inflicted on it by Russia. Tonight, for the first time, the benchmark records harm Ukraine itself caused -- and still holds the score steady.
topSignals.description: Ukraine holds a published score of 50 out of 100, in the Functional band. Ukrainian drones struck two Russian logistics warehouses on July 18, in the Tambov and Moscow regions. The strikes killed nine people across 19 Russian regions and wounded more than 60. Seven of the nine dead were civilian night-shift workers at a retail distribution center in Kotovsk, roughly 500 to 700 kilometers from the front line. President Volodymyr Zelensky publicly acknowledged the strikes and said Ukraine targeted facilities used in drone manufacturing, though no public evidence has been offered for that claim. Every earlier Compassion Benchmark assessment of Ukraine recorded harm inflicted on Ukraine by Russia. This is the first time the benchmark has recorded Ukraine as the country that caused civilian harm, rather than the country that suffered it. Scored against the benchmark's own anchors, tonight's finding is worth about 1.4 points on a 100-point scale -- well short of the 5-point threshold required before a published score changes. The benchmark is now watching for a second such incident, or an international human-rights finding calling the strike unlawful, either of which would move Ukraine's score. Confidence: medium.

evidence.claim: Seven people were killed working at a distribution centre at Kotovsk in the Tambov region; these were night shift workers, and 25 others were wounded.
Ukraine acknowledged carrying out the attacks, with President Volodymyr Zelensky stating that two major logistics facilities were hit in the Moscow and Tambov regions.

topSignals.title: The United States Carries Its Strongest Unconverted Downward Signal in the Country Index
topSignals.whyItMatters: A score set on domestic grounds a month before an Iran strike campaign began has never priced in that campaign -- until tonight's assessment measured it honestly.
topSignals.description: The United States holds a published score of 17.5 out of 100, in the Critical band. That score was set June 9, based on domestic conduct -- in-custody deaths in immigration detention, court rulings against fast-track deportation, and National Guard deployment to five cities. A US strike campaign against Iran began July 6, a month later, and none of that conduct was priced into the score until tonight. The campaign has now run seven consecutive nights of strikes. Iran says at least 50 people have been killed and more than 500 injured since it began, including strikes that hit six bridges near the port city of Bandar Abbas and a desalination plant that left 10,000 people without water. Those casualty figures come from Iran's own Health Ministry and are reported by international outlets as Iranian government claims, not independently verified counts. Scored honestly against the benchmark's anchors, tonight's assessment finds the campaign worth about 2.8 points on a 100-point scale -- still below the 5-point threshold, but the largest unconverted downward signal now open among the countries the benchmark tracks. A court-ordered release of roughly 65,000 immigration detainees is a genuine check by the judiciary on the executive branch, but it is not scored as the executive's own compassionate conduct. Confidence: medium.

evidence.claim: Iran says at least 50 people have been killed and more than 500 injured in strikes by the United States this month, including 5 women and 2 children among the dead.

Officials in Hormozgan province say six bridges were hit along key transport routes linking Bandar Abbas with surrounding towns; two people were killed and eight wounded after strikes hit the Kahurestan Bridge and a residential area.

topSignals.title: Israel Holds at Zero as Gaza City Strikes Kill at Least Nine on Consecutive Days
topSignals.whyItMatters: A second mass-casualty strike in as many days shows the floor score is being actively reinforced by ongoing events, not simply carried forward from the past.
topSignals.description: Israel holds a published score of 0 out of 100, the lowest score the benchmark gives. Israeli strikes in Gaza City on July 18 killed at least nine Palestinians, including three children. A strike on an apartment in the Nasr neighborhood killed five people aged 8 to 18; a separate strike on a gathering in Zeitoun killed four. The Israeli military said it targeted Hamas infrastructure and militants, without detailing the civilian toll. This follows a July 17 strike on a funeral in Nuseirat that killed at least seven and wounded 22. Because Israel is already at the lowest possible score, neither event can lower it further, but both reinforce rather than merely carry forward the floor. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: Israeli strikes in Gaza City on July 18, 2026 killed at least nine Palestinians including three children; a strike on an apartment in the Nasr neighbourhood killed five people aged 8 to 18, and a separate strike on a gathering in Zeitoun killed four.

topSignals.title: Sudan and Russia Both Hold at Zero as Attribution Discipline Runs in Both Directions
topSignals.whyItMatters: The same rule that keeps tonight's Ukraine finding off Russia's ledger also keeps Sudan's floor score resting only on its own conduct, not the war around it.
topSignals.description: Sudan holds a published score of 0 out of 100. The city of El Obeid remains under siege, with roughly 500,000 residents and 105,000 displaced people trapped and food prices up about 300 percent, but no new dated mass-casualty event surfaced this cycle beyond what was already logged. Russia also holds at 0 out of 100. No newly dated Russian-attributed civilian-harm incident was identified this cycle. The most significant dated event on Russian soil this cycle -- the July 18 Kotovsk drone strikes -- is Ukrainian own-conduct under the benchmark's rule that harm counts against whoever inflicts it, so it is logged against Ukraine, not against Russia. Russia's floor rests entirely on its own persistent conduct record: systematic strikes on Ukrainian civilians, documented torture, and intimidation of international courts. Confidence: high for both.

evidence.claim: El Obeid remains under siege by the Rapid Support Forces, with roughly 500,000 residents plus 105,000 displaced people trapped and food prices still up about 300 percent.

topSignals.title: Afghanistan Holds at Zero With Evidence Restored After a Direct Publisher Date Check
topSignals.whyItMatters: After a mis-dated citation left Afghanistan's zero score with no fresh evidence last cycle, tonight's check verified a new report's date directly against the publisher's own web address.
topSignals.description: Afghanistan holds a published score of 0 out of 100. Human Rights Watch documented that more than 17 million Afghans face severe food insecurity in 2026, and said Taliban restrictions on women working in humanitarian roles are worsening the crisis by blocking aid delivery to vulnerable households. Only 20 percent of Afghan households can now afford enough food without aid, down from 28 percent before October 2025. Last cycle, a citation about Afghanistan turned out to be a year old, leaving no verified new evidence behind the score. This cycle, the publication date of the Human Rights Watch report was checked directly against the date encoded in the publisher's own web address, rather than trusted from a secondary aggregator -- the same failure mode that caused last cycle's error. Confidence was raised from low to medium as a result.

evidence.claim: More than 17 million Afghans face severe food insecurity in 2026, and Taliban restrictions on women working in humanitarian and UN roles materially impede aid delivery to vulnerable households.

topSignals.title: Myanmar Holds at Zero as a Claimed Massacre Is Logged as Unverified, Not Scored
topSignals.whyItMatters: A claimed killing of 17 captured soldiers came from a single uncorroborated newsletter. The benchmark logged it as a watch item rather than treating an unverified claim as evidence.
topSignals.description: Myanmar holds a published score of 0 out of 100. The only new evidence available this cycle is a self-published newsletter describing a sunk military supply boat, fighting in Seikphyu Township, and a claimed massacre of 17 captured opposition soldiers by junta forces. No institutional source -- the United Nations, its humanitarian coordination office, Human Rights Watch, or Amnesty International -- corroborated these specific claims within the two-week evidence window. The claimed massacre is recorded as an unverified watch item, not as scored evidence; if institutionally confirmed, it would be a significant new own-conduct finding. Confidence: low, held there deliberately because of the sourcing gap.

evidence.claim: A self-published newsletter reported junta forces killed 17 captured opposition soldiers in Seikphyu Township; no institutional source has corroborated this claim within the evidence window.

topSignals.title: Iran and the Democratic Republic of the Congo Hold Near Zero as Severe Evidence Can No Longer Move Either Score
topSignals.whyItMatters: Both countries sit so close to the bottom of the scale that even severe new evidence -- war strikes in one case, a fast-growing epidemic in the other -- cannot register as a five-point change.
topSignals.description: Iran holds a published score of 2.5 out of 100. The harm from the US strike campaign counts against the United States' own conduct, not Iran's (see above); Iran's own record, including more than 784 executions so far this year, already keeps its score this close to zero. The Democratic Republic of the Congo holds a published score of 2.3 out of 100. Its Ebola outbreak reached 2,124 confirmed cases and 828 to 864 deaths depending on the tracker, still the fastest-growing outbreak on record. The World Health Organization has said the true toll may be roughly double the official count. Because the harm is caused by a disease rather than a documented government failure, and because both scores already sit within roughly two and a half points of the lowest possible score, no evidence this severe can produce the five-point change the benchmark requires before either score moves. Confidence: high for both.

evidence.claim: The DRC's Ebola outbreak reached 2,124 or more confirmed cases and 828 to 864 deaths depending on the tracker, and remains the fastest-growing Ebola outbreak on record.

topSignals.title: Six More Countries Hold Steady as Real Developments Fall Short of a New Escalation
topSignals.whyItMatters: Each of these six countries had a genuine, documented development this cycle, but none supplied evidence of a new failure large enough to move an already-low score.
topSignals.description: Pakistan holds at 17.2 of 100: a security operation in Balochistan has reported 71 to 123 people killed since July 5, overwhelmingly described as militants and reported only by security sources, now unverified for a fourth consecutive week. Lebanon holds at 17.2: a strike on a Hezbollah drone cell counts against Israel, and a Lebanese soldier's death from a roadside device counts as harm done to Lebanon's own forces, not harm Lebanon caused. China holds at 19.5: its Ethnic Unity Law continues to draw international criticism, but no newly dated enforcement action was found this cycle. Venezuela holds at 18: its June 24 earthquake death toll rose to 5,119, but the increase reflects the disaster's scale, not a new government failure -- and the International Monetary Fund released emergency aid this cycle. Cuba holds at 32.8: its record of 1,306 political prisoners and its power-grid crisis were both reaffirmed without a new escalation. South Sudan holds at 0: displacement in Jonglei state continues on an existing severe trajectory, but the operative figures remain dated to March and April, with no fresh escalation found this cycle. Confidence: high across all six.

evidence.claim: The June 24 twin-earthquake death toll in Venezuela climbed to 5,119 killed with more than 16,740 injured; the International Monetary Fund released emergency aid amid the deepening crisis.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Due-process restoration, civil-society reinstatement, and reversal of the indefinite re-election amendment remain the path back toward 20 of 100. The 2027 election under the amendment is the next scheduled test.
boundaryWatch.note: El Salvador sits at 15.0 of 100 in the Critical band, 5.0 points below the boundary with the Developing band. Not assessed tonight; the pattern documented July 16 -- 86 currently held political prisoners and the term-limit amendment remaining in force -- carries forward unchanged.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A confirmed enforcement action under the Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad -- is the next threshold event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: China sits at 19.5 of 100, half a point inside the Critical band. The Ethnic Unity Law continues to draw UN and multi-government criticism over its reach into Taiwan and the diaspora, but no newly dated enforcement action surfaced this cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A second undisclosed telemetry episode, evidence the removed tracker was broader or retained longer than stated, or a regulator or court finding of deceptive practice would each independently move the score toward a change. The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable August 2.
boundaryWatch.note: Anthropic sits at 59.1 of 100, 0.9 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight; carried forward unchanged from the prior cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A labor board ruling on the unfair-labor-practice charge tied to Apple's closure of its first unionized store is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Apple sits at 59.4 of 100, 0.6 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A durable ceasefire combined with the Lebanese Armed Forces taking sovereign control of southern Lebanon and delivering reconstruction would together move Lebanon toward 20 of 100.
boundaryWatch.note: Lebanon sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the boundary with the Developing band. Tonight's dated harm -- a strike on a Hezbollah drone cell, and a Lebanese soldier killed by a roadside device -- is either Israeli-inflicted or non-state-actor harm to Lebanon's own forces, not Lebanese own-conduct.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently verified deliberate targeting of civilians in a cross-border strike would move Pakistan's score down; durable relief for detained protesters and disappeared Balochistan residents would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Pakistan sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the Developing band. A Balochistan security operation's toll continues climbing, still reported only by security sources and unverified into a fourth consecutive week.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independent confirmation of a discrete own-conduct failure, such as documented aid diversion or denial of responder access, is the next event that would move Venezuela's score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Venezuela sits at 18.0 of 100. The earthquake death toll rose materially to 5,119 killed this cycle, but the increase reflects disaster scale; no aid-diversion or responder-denial evidence surfaced.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration in Borno state combined with documented aid obstruction is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Nigeria sits at 18.0 of 100, 2.0 points below the Developing band. Not assessed tonight; the July nutrition-program cut for 300,000+ children carries forward unchanged.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented government obstruction of World Health Organization response corridors would be the trigger to move toward the absolute floor. The natural seasonal peak for this outbreak runs through July 31.
boundaryWatch.note: DRC sits at 2.3 of 100, 2.3 points above the absolute floor. The Ebola outbreak reached 828 to 864 deaths depending on the tracker, still described as the fastest-growing on record.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A documented mass-execution event or formal international atrocity finding is the next event that could move Iran's score toward the absolute floor.
boundaryWatch.note: Iran sits at 2.5 of 100, 2.5 points above the absolute floor. Harm from the seven-day US strike campaign is logged as United States conduct, not scored against Iran.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented expulsions of Bengali Muslim and Rohingya populations, and continued digital surveillance targeting minority groups, remain under watch. Not assessed tonight.
boundaryWatch.note: India sits at 15.6 of 100, 0.6 points above the absolute floor. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration covering Buur Hakaba or Baidoa, combined with documented government aid obstruction, is the next scored trigger. The famine-risk window runs through September 2026.
boundaryWatch.note: Somalia sits at 4.7 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented discriminatory denial of emergency protection to stateless bidoon or migrant workers during wartime would move Kuwait's score down; durable expansion of bidoon rights would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Kuwait sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Mali's conduct against Burkina Faso's (6.3 of 100) is the next scored event needed to resolve this gap. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Mali sits at 12.5 of 100. Not assessed tonight. The calibration question against Burkina Faso has now been open roughly twenty-five consecutive days, with no resolution timeline set. A single-source claim of an Africa Corps helicopter downing was screened this cycle for ambiguous dating.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Burkina Faso's conduct against Mali's (12.5 of 100) is the next scored event needed. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Burkina Faso sits at 6.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight. The calibration question against Mali remains open roughly twenty-five consecutive days.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Bolivia's conduct profile, as an elected government under economic and civil-unrest strain, against Critical-band peers facing state collapse or mass atrocity is the next event needed. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Bolivia sits at 6.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight; the calibration question against Burkina Faso and Somalia remains open, now seven days since the flag was opened July 11.

boundaryWatch.trigger: The outcome of the Justice Department's False Claims Act complaint or the Barrows v. Humana class action over AI-driven claim denials would confirm the current Developing-band placement either way.
boundaryWatch.note: Humana sits at 35.2 of 100, 4.8 points below the Functional band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Forced cuts that measurably reduce student or staff support would count against the score; abandoning its academic-freedom stance would as well.
boundaryWatch.note: Princeton sits at 57.8 of 100, 2.2 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently documented deliberate targeting of Palestinian civilians by Palestinian authorities themselves, or systematic diversion of aid, would move the score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Palestine sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented atrocities by Haitian state security forces, or deliberate relief obstruction, would move the score down. Verified state-led civilian protection would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Haiti sits at 4.7 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A fourth nationwide blackout, or a documented healthcare-access failure beyond tens of thousands of canceled surgeries, is the next event that would move Cuba's score toward a formal change.
boundaryWatch.note: Cuba sits at 32.8 of 100. The grid crisis and the record 1,306 political-prisoner count were both reaffirmed this cycle without a new escalation.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A discrete new harm category beyond documented opposition and press suppression is the next event that would move Turkey's score further down.
boundaryWatch.note: Turkey sits at 10.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A return to lethal crackdown tactics, such as the 41 protester deaths recorded during the 2025 Saba Saba march, would be the next event that moves Kenya's score down toward the Critical band.
boundaryWatch.note: Kenya sits at 35.9 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A documented own-conduct deterioration in epidemic response, such as government obstruction of WHO response corridors, would be the next event that could push Uganda back into the Critical band.
boundaryWatch.note: Uganda sits at 20.3 of 100, just 0.3 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A merits ruling or verdict for the states, a material settlement, or new dated evidence of youth-safety harm or internal-findings suppression is the next event that would move Meta's score.
boundaryWatch.note: Meta Platforms sits at 7.8 of 100, near the bottom of the Fortune 500 list. Not assessed tonight; the August 18 Oakland trial remains the clearest forward trigger.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independent, non-Iranian-state verification of casualty figures; continued civilian-infrastructure targeting past July 18; an institutional unlawfulness finding; or a further domestic detention-death event -- any one of these four would convert this from a confirmation into a proposed score change.
boundaryWatch.note: The United States sits at 17.5 of 100, 2.5 points below the Developing band. Tonight's assessment found the US strike campaign in Iran worth about -2.8 points, its own-conduct baseline never having priced in the campaign; the strongest unconverted downward signal now open in the country index.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A second dated incident of Ukrainian deep-strike civilian casualties, or an institutional finding (OHCHR, HRW, Amnesty) characterizing the July 18 Kotovsk strike as unlawful, would convert this from a confirmation into a proposed score change.
boundaryWatch.note: Ukraine sits at 50.0 of 100. Tonight's assessment recorded the first Ukrainian own-conduct civilian-harm entry in this entity's ledger -- drone strikes that killed seven civilian night-shift workers at Kotovsk -- worth about -1.4 points, held below the 5-point proposal threshold.

recentAssessments.whyHeadline: Ukraine's own strikes killed seven civilian warehouse workers in Russia -- the first such entry against Ukraine itself.

evidence.claim: Seven people were killed working at a distribution centre at Kotovsk in the Tambov region; these were night shift workers, and 25 others were wounded.
Both facilities struck belonged to Wildberries, a major Russian online retailer; the warehouses were located over 500 and nearly 700 kilometres from the front line.

CENTCOM strikes left 10,000 people without water after a desalination plant was hit.

The Kotovsk strikes that hit Russian soil are logged against Ukraine, not credited or debited to Russia.
A Human Rights Watch hunger-crisis report is date-verified directly against the publisher's own web address.

Harm from the US strike campaign counts against the United States, not against Iran's own record.

evidence.claim: Iran says at least 50 people have been killed and more than 500 injured in US strikes this month, following a seventh consecutive night of strikes.

nextForwardSignal.label: The natural seasonal peak for the current Ebola outbreak closes.

evidence.claim: The DRC's Ebola outbreak reached 2,124 or more confirmed cases and 828 to 864 deaths depending on the tracker, and remains the fastest-growing on record.

recentAssessments.whyHeadline: A Balochistan operation's toll keeps climbing, still reported only by security sources into a fourth week.
A strike on a drone cell counts against Israel; a soldier's death counts as harm to Lebanon.
The Ethnic Unity Law keeps drawing criticism, but no newly dated enforcement action was found this cycle.
The earthquake death toll rises to 5,119, but the increase reflects disaster scale, not new conduct.
The grid crisis and the record political-prisoner count are both reaffirmed without a new escalation.

emergingRisks.risk: Ukraine's first own-conduct finding establishes a concrete conversion path: a second dated deep-strike civilian-casualty incident, or an institutional unlawfulness finding, would move the score.
emergingRisks.detail: Tonight's Kotovsk finding was held at roughly -1.4 points, well below the 5-point threshold. The benchmark is now explicitly watching for a repeat incident or an OHCHR, HRW, or Amnesty finding characterizing the strike as unlawful.

emergingRisks.risk: The United States has now carried an unconverted downward signal for two consecutive cycles and is the strongest conversion candidate in the country index.
emergingRisks.detail: Four named triggers could each convert the current -2.8 point finding into a proposal: independent verification of Iranian casualty figures, continued infrastructure targeting, an institutional unlawfulness finding, or a further domestic detention-death event.

emergingRisks.risk: Myanmar's claimed massacre of 17 captured soldiers needs institutional corroboration before it can be scored as evidence.
emergingRisks.detail: The claim currently rests on a single self-published newsletter. If the United Nations, its humanitarian coordination office, Human Rights Watch, or Amnesty International confirm it, the finding would become scoreable own-conduct evidence.

emergingRisks.risk: A structural mismatch between the benchmark's internal scan-prioritization file and the published country index may be steering nightly attention toward the wrong entities.
emergingRisks.detail: A reconciliation check found 154 of 193 country entries recorded in the internal prioritization file disagree with the published index on score, band, or rank -- for example, Finland is recorded there at a composite of 100.0 and rank 1, while the published index shows 84.4 and rank 5. A dedicated reconciliation pass is recommended.

emergingRisks.risk: The Mali-Burkina Faso scoring-consistency question is now open roughly twenty-five consecutive days, the benchmark's longest-running unresolved item.
emergingRisks.detail: Neither country was assessed tonight; the underlying calibration question about the two countries' government-conduct scores remains open with no resolution timeline set.

emergingRisks.risk: The Bolivia critical-band calibration question is now seven days open, alongside the longer-running Mali-Burkina Faso question.
emergingRisks.detail: The question is whether Bolivia's score is calibrated consistently against Critical-band peers facing very different conditions, such as state collapse or mass atrocity.

emergingRisks.risk: The Democratic Republic of the Congo's Ebola outbreak crossed 828 deaths this window, with the natural seasonal peak closing July 31.
emergingRisks.detail: Documented government obstruction of World Health Organization response corridors is the specific trigger that would move the DRC's score toward the absolute floor.

sectorAlerts.sector: Eastern Europe -- Attribution Runs in a New Direction for the First Time
sectorAlerts.headline: Ukraine holds at 50 after its own drone strikes kill civilian workers in Russia; Russia holds at zero, unaffected by the strikes it absorbed.
observations: Ukraine (50.0 of 100): July 18 drone strikes on Russian logistics warehouses killed nine people, seven of them civilian night-shift workers -- the first Ukrainian own-conduct civilian-harm entry in this benchmark's ledger, held below the 5-point threshold at about -1.4 points.
Russia (0 of 100): the Kotovsk strikes that hit Russian soil this cycle are logged against Ukraine, not against Russia; Russia's floor rests on its own persistent conduct record.

sectorAlerts.sector: North America -- A Month-Old Baseline Meets a Two-Week-Old War
sectorAlerts.headline: The United States holds at 17.5 as an Iran strike campaign, never priced into its domestic-grounds score, is assessed for the first time at about -2.8 points.
observations: United States (17.5 of 100): a seven-night US strike campaign against Iran, including strikes on six bridges and a desalination plant, is now the largest unconverted downward signal open in the country index.

sectorAlerts.sector: Middle East -- Two Countries Hold at Zero and Near Zero on Fresh and Attribution-Held Evidence
sectorAlerts.headline: Israel's floor is reinforced by a second consecutive day of mass-casualty strikes; Iran's near-zero score holds as US-strike harm is logged elsewhere.
observations: Israel (0 of 100): Gaza City strikes on July 18 killed at least nine, including three children, a day after a funeral strike killed seven.
Iran (2.5 of 100): harm from the US strike campaign is logged against the United States, not against Iran; Iran's own execution record keeps its score near zero.
Lebanon (17.2 of 100): a July 18 strike on a Hezbollah drone cell and a device that killed a Lebanese soldier are both non-Lebanese-state conduct.

sectorAlerts.sector: South and Central Asia -- A Direct Publisher Date Check Restores Evidence for Afghanistan
sectorAlerts.headline: Afghanistan's zero score gains verified evidence after a Human Rights Watch report's date was checked directly against the publisher's own web address.
observations: Afghanistan (0 of 100): Human Rights Watch documents Taliban restrictions on women's humanitarian work worsening a hunger crisis affecting more than 17 million people.
Pakistan (17.2 of 100): a Balochistan security operation's toll keeps climbing, still reported only by security sources into a fourth consecutive week.

sectorAlerts.sector: Southeast Asia -- A Claimed Massacre Is Held as Unverified, Not Scored
sectorAlerts.headline: Myanmar holds at zero as a single-sourced claim of a 17-soldier massacre is logged as a watch item rather than treated as evidence.
observations: Myanmar (0 of 100): the claim of a junta massacre of captured soldiers came from a self-published newsletter with no institutional corroboration found within the evidence window.

sectorAlerts.sector: Horn of Africa and Sub-Saharan Africa -- Sieges and Outbreaks Continue Without New Escalation
sectorAlerts.headline: Sudan holds at zero as the El Obeid siege continues; the Democratic Republic of the Congo's Ebola outbreak passes 828 deaths.
observations: Sudan (0 of 100): El Obeid remains besieged, with roughly 500,000 residents and 105,000 displaced people trapped; no new dated mass-casualty event surfaced this cycle.
South Sudan (0 of 100): Jonglei displacement continues on its existing trajectory; the operative figures remain dated to March and April.
DRC (2.3 of 100): the Ebola outbreak reached 828 to 864 deaths depending on the tracker, still the fastest-growing on record.

sectorAlerts.sector: East Asia -- A Law's Enforcement Continues Without a New Documented Action
sectorAlerts.headline: China holds at 19.5 as its Ethnic Unity Law remains in force without a newly dated enforcement action this cycle.
observations: China (19.5 of 100): the law continues to draw UN and multi-government criticism over its reach into Taiwan and the diaspora.

sectorAlerts.sector: Latin America and the Caribbean -- A Rising Earthquake Toll and a Static Grid Crisis Both Hold Steady
sectorAlerts.headline: Venezuela's earthquake toll rises to 5,119 as Cuba's grid crisis and political-prisoner record are both reaffirmed without new escalation.
observations: Venezuela (18.0 of 100): the June 24 earthquake death toll climbed to 5,119; the International Monetary Fund released emergency aid this cycle.
Cuba (32.8 of 100): the July 14 blackout and the record 1,306 political-prisoner count were both reaffirmed, with no new escalation identified.

sectorAlerts.sector: Sahel -- A Scoring-Consistency Question Passes Roughly Three and a Half Weeks Open
sectorAlerts.headline: Neither Mali nor Burkina Faso was assessed tonight, leaving their scoring-consistency question unresolved at roughly twenty-five days.
observations: Mali (12.5 of 100) and Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100) were both carried forward unassessed; a single-source claim of a helicopter downing in Mali was screened for ambiguous dating.
The question of whether the two countries are scored consistently for comparable government conduct remains open, with no resolution timeline set.

methodologyNotes.name: Attribution Can Run Against the Party the Public Usually Sees as the Victim
methodologyNotes.description: The benchmark scores harm against whichever country's forces carried it out, not against whichever country is more sympathetic in a given conflict. Tonight, for the first time, that rule produced a finding against Ukraine rather than against Russia: Ukrainian drone strikes killed civilian workers inside Russia, and the benchmark recorded that as Ukraine's own conduct, exactly as it has recorded Russian strikes on Ukraine as Russia's own conduct in every earlier cycle.

methodologyNotes.name: A Score Can Sit Unpriced for Weeks If a New Conduct Stream Begins After the Baseline Was Set
methodologyNotes.description: When a country's published score is set on one basis -- for example, domestic conduct -- and a separate, unrelated conduct stream begins afterward -- for example, a foreign military campaign -- that new conduct is not automatically reflected until it is specifically assessed. The United States' score was set June 9 on domestic grounds; its Iran strike campaign, which began a month later, was assessed for the first time tonight.

methodologyNotes.name: A Sub-Threshold Finding Is Tracked Toward Conversion, Not Set Aside
methodologyNotes.description: When new evidence produces a real but small score movement, the benchmark records the exact magnitude, states clearly why it falls below the 5-point proposal threshold, and names the specific evidence that would convert it into a proposal. Tonight's Ukraine and United States findings both follow this pattern: real, measured, sub-threshold, and explicitly tracked.

methodologyNotes.name: Source Quality Is Checked Even When a Claim Would Otherwise Be a Significant Finding
methodologyNotes.description: A claim that would be a significant new own-conduct finding -- such as a reported massacre of captured soldiers -- is still held at low confidence and excluded from scoring if it comes from a single, non-institutional source. Tonight's Myanmar claim was logged as an unverified watch item rather than scored, regardless of its severity, because no institutional source had corroborated it.

methodologyNotes.name: A Publication Date Should Be Checked Against the Publisher's Own Record, Not an Aggregator
methodologyNotes.description: When a prior cycle's date error traced back to trusting a secondary aggregator's framing, the following cycle's evidence is checked directly against the primary publisher's own dating -- for example, the date encoded in a report's web address on the publisher's own domain. Afghanistan's evidence this cycle was verified this way, directly repairing the specific failure mode that caused an error last cycle.

forwardTriggers.trigger: The natural seasonal peak for the current Ebola outbreak closes. Documented government obstruction of WHO response corridors is the trigger toward the absolute floor.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable. A second undisclosed telemetry episode or a regulator finding of deceptive practice would be the next event that could move the score.
The Oakland youth-safety trial is the next event that could turn the $1.4 trillion penalty demand into an adjudicated finding.
The famine-risk window closes. A formal famine declaration combined with documented government aid obstruction would move the score.
The 2027 election under the July 2025 indefinite re-election amendment. Restoring due process and reinstating civil society are the conditions that would move the score back up.
A second dated incident of Ukrainian deep-strike civilian casualties, or an institutional finding characterizing the Kotovsk strike as unlawful, would convert tonight's confirmation into a proposal.

Independent verification of Iranian casualty figures, continued infrastructure targeting, an institutional unlawfulness finding, or a further domestic detention-death event would each convert tonight's confirmation into a proposal.
Institutional corroboration (UN, OCHA, HRW, or Amnesty) of the claimed massacre of 17 captured soldiers would convert this from an unverified watch item into scoreable evidence.
Any formal international sanctions listing against the Rapid Support Forces would be logged against the group, not against Sudan's own score.
A documented mass-execution event or formal international atrocity finding is the next event that could move Iran's score toward the absolute floor.
Coordinator-level review of Mali's score against Burkina Faso's for comparable military-government conduct. Roughly twenty-five days open with no set review date.
The same coordinator-level review, from Burkina Faso's side of the calibration question.
Coordinator-level review of whether Bolivia's score is calibrated consistently against Critical-band peers facing very different conditions. No review date has been set.
First confirmed enforcement action under the Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad.
A fourth nationwide blackout, or a documented healthcare-access failure beyond tens of thousands of canceled surgeries, is the next event that would move Cuba's score toward a formal change.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: Ukraine's Kotovsk strikes (-1.4pt) and the United States' Iran campaign (-2.8pt) both cleared attribution under the acting-party rule but stayed below the 5-point proposal threshold. Does the same fixed-point threshold, applied to conduct of very different scale, produce comparable outcomes across entities, or does it require calibration by dimension?
dailyOpeningQuestion.tensionFraming: Two entities carry real, newly-priced own-conduct findings this cycle, both held below threshold by the same rule.
